original_abstract: Precise adjustment of metal-organic framework (MOF) architectures at sub-angstrom scales is a powerful method for enhancing gas separation, yet its impact often diminishes in materials with larger pores. Here, we present an integrative design strategy that amplifies the effects of ultrafine pore tuning by first inducing extreme pore compression. This approach utilizes the multi-modular partitioned acs (pacs) platform, which exhibits high tolerance for isoreticular substitution. By employing a bioisosteric replacement strategy—substituting the standard 1,4-benzenedicarboxylate with trans-1,3-cyclobutanedicarboxylate (tcb)—we achieved a massive 30% reduction in the unit-cell c/a ratio. This "extreme compression" established a confined environment where subsequent ultrafine tuning (less than 3% variation) via a second ligand module became highly influential. Consequently, the C2H2/CO2 selectivity increased dramatically from 2.6 to 20.8, a performance confirmed by experimental gas breakthrough studies. This work demonstrates that the use of small, cyclic dicarboxylates like tcb provides a significant new route for sub-angstrom engineering and high-efficiency gas purification.
humanized_abstract: We have shown that precise adjustment of the structure of metal-organic frameworks at the sub-angstrom level is highly effective as a means to increase gas selectivity, yet often little effect is noted for larger pore structures. In this study, an integrative design approach consisting of a sequence of ultraprecise structural adjustments (USAs) in MOFs has been successfully realized using the multi-modular partitioned acs (pacs) platform that can tolerate significant numbers of isoreticular substitutions and functional variations. Bioisosteric substitutions using 1,4-benzenedicarboxylate (BDC) with the small, cyclic dicarboxylate ligand, trans-1,3-cyclobutanedicarboxylate (tcb) resulted in the increase of the c/a ratio in the unit cell by a remarkable 30% through the expansion of the μ4-O2TCF bridges, providing an "extremely compressed" pore structure. Further adjusting the structure of the resulting compressed MOF only by less than 3% via a second series of USAs using a smaller ligand led to an increase in C2H2/CO2 selectivity from 2.6 to 20.8 and confirmed further through the gas breakthrough studies. The use of small, cyclic dicarboxylates, such as tcb, thus opens an entirely new avenue for sub-angstrom structural adjustments in MOFs for highly selective gas separations.

Further adjusting the structure of the resulting compressed MOF only by less than 3% via a second series of USAs using a smaller ligand led to an increase in C2H2/CO2 selectivity from 2.6 to 20.8 and confirmed further through the gas breakthrough studies.
